title: Salesforce's Agentforce Just Hit $1B in ARR. The Stock's Down 35% Anyway.
subtitle: Marc Benioff's 'digital labor revolution' is the fastest product ramp in Salesforce history — but Wall Street is pricing CRM like a dying utility, not an AI breakout.
summary: Salesforce (CRM) crossed $1B in Agentforce ARR in under a year — the fastest product ramp in company history. Revenue re-accelerated to 13% YoY, free cash flow hit $16.6B, and forward PE sits at an absurd 11x. Yet the stock is down 35% from its 52-week high. Here's why the disconnect between Salesforce's AI momentum and its valuation is the most interesting setup in enterprise tech right now.
bodyHtml:
Let's get this out of the way upfront: Salesforce just posted $1 billion in Agentforce annual recurring revenue in under twelve months. That's the fastest product ramp in the company's 27-year history — almost triple where it was a single quarter ago. And the stock? Sitting at $171, down 35% from its 52-week high, priced like a legacy utility that forgot how to grow. Something doesn't add up.

You're looking at a company doing $41.5 billion in revenue — up 9.6% last year, accelerating to 13% this quarter — printing $16.6 billion in free cash flow, with gross margins north of 77% and a forward P/E of 11 times. Eleven. For a business whose AI product grew 100% quarter-over-quarter to hit a billion-dollar ARR run rate. The PEG ratio is 0.78. That's not value territory — that's the market handing you a discount on a growth AI company and daring you to take it.

Here's the part that should make you lean in: Agentforce isn't some beta experiment or slide-deck vision. It's processing 28.6 trillion tokens per quarter — up 152% from the prior quarter. 9,500-plus paid deals. 3.8 billion agentic work units delivered for customers in a single quarter. PenFed Credit Union deployed 76 AI agents and cut call-handle time by 10%, after-call work by 50%, saving roughly $1.6 million a year. UCLA Health built its first customer-facing Agentforce deployment in eight months. This is real, it's in production, and it's accelerating.

Marc Benioff calls it the digital labor revolution — a $3 to $12 trillion opportunity where companies build limitless digital workforces using autonomous AI agents. He's invoked the Japanese concept of shoshin — beginner's mind — to avoid the innovator's dilemma that killed the very CRM companies Salesforce disrupted. He's rebranded every cloud product under the Agentforce umbrella. Acquired Informatica for $8 billion to own the data layer. Bought Fin for $3.6 billion to own the AI customer service front end. Set a $60 billion revenue target for fiscal 2030 and committed $50 billion in buybacks. This is not a management team playing defense.

The competitive landscape makes the story even more interesting. Microsoft Copilot Studio has the M365 ecosystem going for it — SharePoint, Teams, Outlook, Azure. It's a natural fit if your whole company already lives in Microsoft's world. ServiceNow leads on IT service management and internal workflow automation, with Now Assist purpose-built for behind-the-firewall agents. But Salesforce has something neither of those can replicate: the deepest CRM data set on earth, unified through Data Cloud, grounded through the proprietary Atlas Reasoning Engine, and accessible to any AI model via the Headless 360 MCP protocol. Your customer data is your moat. And nobody has better customer data than Salesforce.

You don't need to take Benioff's word for it. The adoption numbers speak for themselves. 66% of organizations are now using agentic AI — up from 39% last year. 89% of those who've deployed AI agents say they want to expand. And 77% are deploying agents for both customer-facing and internal use cases. This isn't a niche — it's a platform shift, and Salesforce is sitting on the best distribution channel in enterprise software with 150,000-plus customers and a 99% retention rate.

So why is the stock down 35%? The market hates uncertainty more than it loves growth. Revenue decelerated from 20%-plus to around 9%, and even though it's re-accelerating on AI tailwinds, the market is skeptical about monetization timelines. KeyBanc downgraded on concerns Agentforce was failing to gain momentum — right before the product doubled ARR in a single quarter. That's the nature of being early. But at 11 times forward earnings with a 0.78 PEG ratio and $16.6 billion in free cash flow, you're not paying for optimism. You're paying for the part of the story the market hasn't processed yet.

Agentforce hit $1 billion in ARR faster than any product Salesforce has ever built. The digital labor market is measured in trillions. Benioff's $60 billion FY2030 target implies a 10% CAGR that AI could easily accelerate past. And you can buy this story at 11x earnings. Sometimes the market hands you a discount and calls it a risk. The question is whether you're brave enough to take it.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in CRM. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
